candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This now ranks first because the user explicitly wants more aggressive post-selection implementations instead of another micro-retime.
The current profile still leaves enormous utilization headroom, so geometry and staging changes should outrank tiny PTX-local tweaks.
Among the live families already in-tree, this is the strongest structural branch with credible multi-millisecond upside.
candidates.hypothesis: The current PTX winner surface still sits at only about 48% tensor-pipe activity, 47.8% SM throughput, and 16.6% active warps. Under the new human guidance, that means the next recommendation should not be another narrow PTX-local retime whose upside is measured in hundredths of a millisecond. The strongest aggressive live family is to promote the existing 256x128 pivot hot-band kernel and give the hot region a materially different reuse geometry. That branch is the cleanest in-tree candidate for recovering multi-millisecond headroom because it changes the hot-band shape, staging footprint, and control amortization all at once rather than chasing another tiny local ordering tweak.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Structural latency hiding and control amortization on the hot band due to undersized effective work per scheduling decision, not just one more PTX microkernel handoff seam.
candidates.risk: Medium to high. This is a broad hot-band dispatch change, but that is now a feature rather than a bug because the user explicitly wants more aggressive implementations once a family is chosen.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.171520 ms restored PTX run
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 promotion does not materially improve utilization or if it opens a broad regression without any sign of structural headroom capture.

candidates.direction_name: Port grouped-row traversal into the non-PTX 128x128 sibling
ranking_notes: This stays above any remaining PTX-local family because it represents a true alternate execution surface.
It ranks below dir_01 because the 256x128 pivot is the larger structural move with the clearest profile-driven rationale.
It remains important for queue breadth under the user's new aggressive-search rule.
candidates.hypothesis: If the loop is going to take a big swing, it should also preserve one alternate 128x128 surface that leaves the PTX microkernel entirely. Porting grouped-row traversal into the non-PTX 128x128 sibling does exactly that: it keeps the overall hot-band footprint but changes the execution surface and consumer pattern more fundamentally than another PTX control tweak. It ranks second because the 256x128 pivot is the more aggressive and more direct geometry change, but this sibling-surface branch still aligns with the new strategy far better than any sub-0.1 ms PTX-local chase.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Surface-level control and locality inefficiency on the current PTX hot-band path rather than one more within-surface handoff detail.
candidates.risk: Medium. This is a real alternate surface jump, but it still stays within a familiar 128x128 regime and better matches the new aggressive-search guidance than another PTX-local tweak.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the sibling surface remains clearly behind the PTX baseline without showing any utilization or locality advantage worth following.

candidates.direction_name: Retune The Auxiliary 256x128 Hot-Band K-Loop Schedule
ranking_notes: This keeps another aggressive 256x128 family live under the new guidance.
It ranks below dir_01 and dir_02 because it is a follow-on schedule family rather than the cleanest first structural jump.
It still deserves the third slot because the user explicitly wants less timid implementations after selection.
candidates.hypothesis: The queue should still carry a second 256x128-oriented family that can follow immediately if the direct 256x128 pivot promotion shows signal but misses cleanly. The auxiliary 256x128 hot-band K-loop schedule family is more aggressive than the current PTX control-path tweaks because it assumes the search should shift into broader geometry and schedule rewrites once a family is chosen. It stays third because the first two directions are even cleaner structural jumps, but it is still more aligned with the new human guidance than any remaining micro-optimization family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: K-loop scheduling and latency hiding on the broader 256x128 hot-band regime rather than PTX-local handoff or export sequencing.
candidates.risk: Medium. This is a schedule-heavy 256x128 branch, but it keeps the search focused on structural paths with real upside.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the auxiliary 256x128 family cannot show a credible utilization improvement or if it degenerates into another tiny local tweak.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
